straight_beat.name: Chet Pazulski
straight_beat.column:
The Pirates’ 4-3 win over the Cubs yesterday was the kind of game that makes you lean forward in your chair, fingers drumming a rhythm only a baseball fan could decipher. Cruz doubled, Skenes struck out ten, and the Pirates beat the Cubs 3-1 at PNC Park. Record now 7-9. Wait, no—hold on. That’s yesterday’s game. The record is 9-5. Yeah, that’s right. The Pirates are 9-5, -4 in the NL Central. Yinzer math, boss.  

So here’s the thing: this team isn’t just winning games. They’re winning games against teams that should be ahead of them. The Cubs, for all their midseason swoons, are still a team that’s been competitive. The Reds? They’re a mess. The Brewers? They’re a mess. The Cardinals? They’re a mess. And the Pirates? They’re in the thick of it. That’s not a coincidence. It’s a result of a front office that’s finally stopped pretending they’re rebuilding and started acting like they’re competing.  

Skenes, that rookie pitcher

optimist.name: Franny Harsanyi
optimist.column:
Paul Skenes threw 102 and I almost called my daughter to come over and watch. That’s the feeling that comes back. That’s why we keep showing up.  

You know, I’ve been thinking a lot about Steve Blass lately. Not the guy who won 20 games in 1971, but the spirit he left behind—the kind of pitcher who made you believe in the magic of a baseball game, even when the odds weren’t in your favor. Paul Skenes isn’t just throwing 102 mph; he’s channeling that same old-school grit, the kind that made Blass a legend. And if you’re wondering why I’m comparing him to Smoky Burgess, well, let’s just say Smoky had a way of making the impossible seem routine. Skenes is doing that now, with a fastball that’s got the same kind of zip that made Burgess a star in the 60s. It’s not just the velocity—it’s the confidence, the poise, the way he’s making every hitter feel like they’re facing a ghost.  

The Pirates’ 4-3 win over the Cubs was a reminder that this team isn’t done yet. Sure, they’re still a game behind in the NL Central, but you can’t measure a season by a single game or even a handful of them. You measure it by moments like this—when the bullpen steps in, when the offense finds its rhythm, when the fans start to remember what it feels like to believe again. The Pirates have that kind of heart. They’ve got the kind of heart that makes you want to raise the Jolly Roger, n’at.  

I’ll admit it’s frustrating to
